name: Thepyrgos
description:
Thepyrgos

Key Demographics: Humans

Capital City: Thepyrgos (The City)

Entanglements

To the North:
Viterra – The Black River is a legal and physical border; hedge-law meets Synod edict here. Fugitives cross both ways; the Chain-Lanterns watch the fords.
To the East:
The Dolmis Sea – Zakov corsairs and Aelinnel tide-traders slip past the chain-towers. The boom is raised when the moon is high and the tide is low.
To the South:
The Hyro Hinterlands – Astroegro ruins leak forgotten magic; the Commissariat sweeps the villages for latent talent. The Ninth's Coterie moves freely in the ruins.
To the West (via the Amaranthine):
Kahfagia – Signal-codes and beacon treaties are exchanged; the Lantern-Law and the Synod maintain a watchful peace across the. Neither trusts the other's mirrors.

Quick Reference

Tagline: Every stair is a question. Every answer is a proof. The Ninth and the Witness argue over whose record holds the truth.

The Archon (Elite):

“Every stair climbed is a word in the great conversation between earth and sky. This city is not built—it is grown, stone by stone, law by law, until the very architecture speaks wisdom. But the stairs remember more than the living. They remember the ones who never came down.”

Bell-Runner (Commoner):

“I know every step that creaks a warning and every landing that whispers gossip. The bells tell the city's mood better than any lord—three chimes means business, six means trouble, and nine means the hunters are coming. But on the ninth bell, the stairs themselves answer.”

Genre / Mood: Scholarly vertical city, labyrinthine imperial bureaucracy, coastal peninsular folk horror in the hinterlands, metaphysical war between memory and forgetting.

Starting Location: The Synod Hall at dusk. Gold-glass mosaics glitter as a Matriarch reads a scroll aloud. “The Beacon Crown sees traitors in the smoke. A child's talent was surrendered to the Commissary last night. Find the informer—or the next stair seized will be yours. And if you hear a stair creak where none exists, do not turn around. The Ninth is counting.”

Theme & Atmosphere

Thepyrgos is both realm and city, built on the bones of the drowned Astroegro Empire. The capital rises in tiers of towers, terraces, and endless stairs above the Dolmis shore—but the stairs are not stone. They are fossilized vertebrae, the spine of a leviathan that Khemesh dragged under when the last emperor broke a covenant older than the sea. Once a Utaran province, now a nation unto itself, Thepyrgos is famed for its universities, libraries, and debating synods, where precedent and philosophy weigh as much as pikes or coin.

The Astroegro were a human civilization of formidable magic, the ancient rivals of the Valewood's Empire of Leaves. Their ruins litter the hills and swamps south of the city, and their legacy haunts every threshold. When the Empire of Leaves fell, Lethai-thora refugees—high elves who had served as archivists and lore-keepers—fled across the Black River and were granted sanctuary in Thepyrgos. Generations later, their descendants still walk the city's tiers, their silver hair and ink-stained fingers a quiet reminder of a pact older than the synod. Many now hold chairs at the university, tend the forbidden archives, or secretly serve the Witness or the Ninth.

Outside the city, the countryside is a rugged coastal landscape of limestone hills, olive groves, and whitewashed villages—but the weather is warmer, wetter, and the valleys are choked with reed-filled marshes. This is the land of the Hyro people, the surviving descendants of the Astroegro. The Hyro carry the fracture of their ancestors in their blood. A child born in the hills might dream in geometries that do not exist. A farmer might hear the stones whispering in a language no living throat can shape. The Chain-Lanterns and other Witch-Hunter Commissariats ruthlessly patrol these villages, using posted warrants, candle-smoke tests, and stair-seizures to identify and kidnap children with arcane talent. The Hyro have learned to offer substitutes—a memory, a false name, a lock of hair—at crossroads, hoping the Hollow accepts the tribute.

In the university's forbidden tiers, the Witness and the Ninth fight a proxy war via fraternal orders and occult mystery cults. The Witness wants the Astroegro Vaults remembered but sealed; the Ninth wants them opened, to trigger a Great Unlearning that would erase the concept of record from the world. No provenance would remain. The Hollow would go unanswered. The Witness would have nothing to inscribe. The Ninth would become the only law: forgetting. No one is sure which side the Archon secretly favors. And in the ruins of the Astroegro, something still ticks—gears of black glass, locked doors that hum, and the occasional survivor with eyes like burning tar who speaks of a pressure from below.

What you notice first: The endless chime of bells from different tiers, never quite in sync. The smell of brine, olive oil, and candle wax. The way locals glance at the sky when a child coughs—and then at the nearest crossroads. The quiet grace of the Lethai-thora archivists, who never seem to blink.

The one rule that kills newcomers: Never let a bell-ringer count your breaths. The Chain-Lanterns offer safe conduct to those who give names that ring false on the test stairs. But worse: never ask a question of a stair that has no end. The Ninth is listening, and it will answer.

Regional Mood: Stairs, Bells, and the Argument of Patrons

Here, every ascent is a plea and every descent a confession. Thepyrgos the city is a vertical labyrinth of stair-rights, rope-guilds, and synod edicts that shift with the tide. Thepyrgos the province is a haunted countryside of hidden talent and state-sanctioned abduction. The Hyro keep their arcane children in root cellars and haylofts, praying the hunters take the neighbor's instead. The Chain-Lanterns move at dawn, their brass censers swinging, their bells tuned to the frequency of latent magic. In the university's forbidden tiers, the Witness and the Ninth arm rival fraternities with citations and curses, and no one is sure which side the Archon secretly favors. The Lethai-thora maintain the great archives, but they are divided—some serve the Witness as faithful recorders, others whisper to the Ninth in darkened scriptoria. And in the ruins of the Astroegro, something still ticks—gears of black glass, locked doors that hum, and the occasional survivor with eyes like burning tar who speaks of a pressure from below.

Geographic Integration

Thepyrgos is landlocked on three sides by Viterra, separated by the Black River and the Belmont Marshes. The marshy lowlands breed strange fogs and rarer creatures, and the border is porous—Viterran hedge-courts and Thepyrgos synods argue jurisdiction over every flooded farmstead. To the northeast, a short boat ride from Fairport (Viterra) across the Belworth estuary brings travelers to Marcott in Vhasia, making Thepyrgos a quiet but crucial link in the riverine trade between the fractured sun-kingdom and the imperial remnant. The city itself controls the Astroegro Straits—the narrow sea passage between the Dolmis and the Amaranthine—and maintains a few small colonies on the western coast of Hyaar, where olive oil and wine are exchanged for eastern silks and spices. Those colonies are run by exiled scholars and retired Chain-Lanterns, and they are said to be even more paranoid than the mother city.

Faction Timers (Thepyrgos)

The politics of Thepyrgos are a delicate balance between four powers: the Archon's civil authority, the Synod's religious law, the Commissariat's witch-hunters, and the hidden war between the Witness and the Ninth.

  • Archon's Balance [6] — Tracks the Archon's ability to keep the synod, guilds, and Commissariat in check.

    Advances when: the party exposes a Commissariat secret (+1), a Synod edict is defied (+1), or a faction gains too much influence (+1).

    At 4 segments: the Archon's authority frays; all social rolls involving civic authority gain +1 DV.

    At 6 segments: the Archon loses control of one faction—either the Commissariat runs wild, the Synod calls for his deposition, or the Ninth's Coterie operates openly. The party must choose which faction to support.

  • Commissariat Pressure [4] — Tracks the Chain-Lanterns' hunting intensity.

    Advances when: the party uses unsanctioned magic in the city, helps a gifted child escape, or defies a Commissary warrant (+1).

    At 4 segments: the Commissariat conducts a city-wide sweep. All PCs must test Spirit+Resolve (DV 4) or be detained for questioning. A detainee may be released for 1 Boon or a favor to the Commissariat.

  • Ninth's Progress [8] — Tracks the Ninth's Coterie's efforts to open the Astroegro Vaults.

    Advances when: the party opens a vault (+2), speaks a true name of an Astroegro ruin (+1), or accepts a boon from the Ninth (+1).

    At 4 segments: the Coterie finds a vault entrance. At 8 segments: they open a vault. The Unfinished Stair appears in the city. The party must decide: help them, stop them, or seal the vault themselves.

  • Witness's Vigil [6] — Tracks the Witness's Archivists' efforts to record and seal dangerous knowledge.

    Advances when: the party records a truth in the Synod's archives (+1), seals a vault (+2), or refuses a boon from the Ninth (+1).

    At 4 segments: the Witness's Archivists offer the party a boon (a free Synod Indulgence or Archive Hour). At 6 segments: they demand a favor—record a dangerous truth or face their scrutiny.

Faction Relationship Dial: Witness ↔ Ninth

Track the party's perceived allegiance between the Witness (memory, record, preservation) and the Ninth (forgetting, erasure, revelation).

  • Witness-leaning:
    • +1 Position on social rolls with archivists, Synod clerks, and the Matriarch
    • The Chain-Lanterns view you as potential informants (first interrogation starts at +1 Position)
    • The Ninth's Coterie actively works against you (complications involving forgotten records or hidden stairs)
  • Ninth-leaning:
    • +1 Effect when seeking hidden knowledge, forbidden texts, or vaults
    • The Commissariat views you as dangerous (first contact starts Desperate)
    • Archivists are wary of you; the Synod may call you to account
  • Centering actions:
    • Record a truth in the Synod's archives (move toward Witness)
    • Feed a memory to the Ninth's Coterie (move toward Ninth)
    • Perform a ritual that acknowledges both (center the dial, clear 1 segment from Unfinished Stair)

Prominent NPCs of Thepyrgos

Name

Role/TitleMotivationRumored Quirk
Archon KonstantinosRuler of the city, elected by the wardsBalance the synod, the guilds, and the CommissariatHe wears a key around his neck; no one knows what it opens—or that it opens the Ninth's Vault.
Matriarch EleniSynod primate, head of the Temple of Light's synodKeep the Everflame from reclaiming the provinceShe speaks once a session; when she does, the bells listen. She has never spoken the ninth word.
Commissary KyrillosChain-Lantern commanderPurge all untutored magic from the provinceHis lantern shows not only latent talent but also the bearer's greatest fear. He refuses to look into it himself.
SofiaBell-Runner (commoner exemplar)Outrun the hunters and protect the hidden giftedShe ties a knot in her hair for each hunter she has outrun; her hair is a thicket. One knot is white—a hunter she could not outrun.
Nomophylax ZoeLaw-scholar, master of citationsReform the synod from withinShe has never lost a case; her opponents have a habit of vanishing into the Unfinished Stair.
The Lighthouse-PatriarchReclusive seer of the Beacon CrownSteer the city through the coming stormHe has no eyes—only mirrors where his eyes should be. In the mirrors you see the faces of those whose names the Ninth holds.
Keeper Lian (Lethai-thora)High Archivist of the Library of KeysPreserve the Astroegro records and keep the Ninth's cult from the deepest vaultsShe has not spoken aloud in twenty years. She writes on water. The water never freezes.

The Archon's Key

The Archon wears a key around his neck. No one knows what it opens. The key is a stair.

  • What it opens: The Ninth Vault—the deepest of the Astroegro vaults, sealed by the first Archon. It contains the original Astroegro orrery, a working model of the cosmos that still ticks.
  • What happens if the party uses it:
    • The vault opens. The Ninth's Coterie is immediately aware.
    • The Witness's Archivists arrive within the hour.
    • The party must choose: destroy the vault's contents (gain the Witness's favor), use them (gain the Ninth's favor), or seal it again (gain the Archon's favor—and a permanent enemy in whichever faction they defied).
  • How the party can learn this:
    • A dying Archivist whispers it (cost: 1 SB)
    • The Lighthouse-Patriarch shows them in a mirror (cost: 1 Fatigue)
    • The Ninth's Coterie offers to trade the information (cost: 1 Obligation to the Ninth)

The Lighthouse-Patriarch's Mirror

Once per session, a character who spends a scene in the Lighthouse-Patriarch's presence may ask the GM one question about:

  • A hidden door or stair in the city
  • The location of an Astroegro vault entrance
  • A weakness of the Chain-Lanterns or the Ninth's Coterie

Cost: The mirrors show the asker a face they have wronged. Mark 1 Fatigue (guilt).

Limit: The Patriarch will not answer the same question twice. He does not need to speak—the mirrors show the answer. If the party asks a question the Patriarch cannot answer, the mirrors show a blank expanse of water.

Names of Thepyrgos

Inspired by the southern peninsulas and the Aegean coast: clear vowels, soft endings, and surnames that recall a stair, a tower, or a village. Lethai-thora names are longer, more flowing, often ending in -iel, -ael, -ir.
